findings.message: `into_parts()` still returns only `(Crate, BTreeMap<Id, ItemAction>)`, so any `ExtendedCrate` built with `with_impl_owner_map()` silently loses the new orphan-ownership metadata when it is decomposed. That makes the new field non-round-trippable and can break callers that rely on `into_parts()` to preserve all state.

Removing the external-`for_` filter admits cross-crate impls into a key space that still uses only the bare `for_` short name. If a crate has both a local type and an external type with the same last path segment implementing the same trait (for example, `impl Printable for Error` and `impl Printable for std::error::Error`), the `BTreeMap<String, Id>` will silently collapse one impl and phase 2 can evaluate the wrong impl set.

P1: [mod.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/mod.rs#L389) The new `for_path_raw` tiebreaker assumes the raw `Type::ResolvedPath.path` is identical on S and C, but this map also contains A-sourced impls whose `for_.path` is synthesized from catalogue short names rather than rustdoc’s original module-qualified path. On a same-key collision that mixes A-sourced and rustdoc-sourced impls, S and C can still sort different winners and produce a false `CMinusSUnionD` red.

P1: [tests.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs#L2716) The new collision regression test does not exercise the changed behavior: both competing impl fixtures use the same `for_.path` (`"MyError"`), so the assertion only checks the final `Id` fallback. A regression in the new path-based ordering would still pass this test.

The new `(key, for_path_raw, id)` tiebreaker relies on the verbatim `Type::ResolvedPath.path` string, but this field is not canonicalized anywhere in the function. If C-side rustdoc and the S-side/orphan-impl data spell the same type differently (for example `Foo` vs `ext_crate::Foo`), the surviving impl for a colliding key can still differ between sides, which reintroduces the false `CMinusSUnionD` mismatch this change is meant to eliminate.

`BoundOp::Equal` is still being dropped here: every `WherePredicateDecl` is emitted as `WherePredicate::BoundPredicate`, and the `operator` field is only read to silence the warning. That means a catalogue decoded from `"operator": "Equal"` will silently turn back into a `:` predicate when it is converted to the extended crate, which breaks round-tripping and changes semantics.

The new `operator` field is not validated for `Equal` predicates. This boundary only rejects empty `rhs`, so `operator = Equal` with zero or multiple RHS entries is still accepted and then re-serialized by the encoder, leaving illegal domain states representable even though `WherePredicateDecl` documents `Equal` as a single-RHS form.
`rhs` is still validated with `validate_bound_str()`, which parses `TypeParamBound`. That rejects valid equality RHS values like `u32`, so the new `operator: "Equal"` form cannot actually be decoded.

`BoundOp::Equal` is serialized without enforcing `rhs.len() == 1`, so a malformed in-memory predicate can round-trip into invalid JSON, and the extended-crate codec will silently drop every RHS entry after the first. This is a data-loss regression for the new schema.

`CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodec` now handles `BoundOp::Equal`, but it never re-validates that the domain value actually has exactly one RHS element. The branch at [catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec.rs:946](./libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec.rs#L946) takes `.first()` and silently drops any extra entries, so an in-memory `WherePredicateDecl { operator: Equal, rhs: [U, V] }` is corrupted into `where ... = U` instead of failing.

The new `Equal` branch accepts any RHS string that parses as a `TypeRef`, but it does not reject RHS forms that `parse_type_ref_str` cannot preserve, such as qualified paths (`<T as Trait>::Assoc`). `decode.rs` will accept those inputs via `validate_type_ref_str` at [decode.rs:434](./libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_document_codec/decode.rs#L434), and `catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec.rs` then silently degrades them to unresolved placeholders at [catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec.rs:968](./libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec.rs#L968), breaking round-trip fidelity.

The new `BoundOp::Equal` path is not exercised by any regression test. The added codec tests cover legacy decode, new-format decode, and Bound-only JSON emission, but they never verify `where_predicate_decl_to_dto` or the `EqPredicate` branch in `CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodec`, so a break in single-RHS equality serialization would slip past CI.

`operator: Equal` only validates that `rhs.len() == 1`; it does not validate that `lhs` is an associated-type projection. A catalogue entry such as `{ "lhs": "T", "rhs": ["U"], "operator": "Equal" }` decodes successfully even though `where T = U` is not valid Rust, and the extended-crate encoder will emit an `EqPredicate` with a bare `Type::Generic` LHS. Please reject Equal predicates whose LHS is not a supported projection form such as `T::Assoc`, so invalid external input cannot become a nonsensical `ExtendedCrate`.

`BoundOp::Equal` only checks `rhs.len() == 1` before serializing. The decoder added an additional invariant that Equal predicates must have a projection LHS such as `T::Assoc`, so a domain value constructed directly with `lhs = "T"`, `rhs = ["u32"]`, `operator = Equal` will encode successfully to JSON that this same codec rejects on decode. This breaks the advertised round-trip behavior for domain-to-DTO conversion; mirror the decoder's Equal-LHS validation here before returning `BoundOpDto::Equal`.

The ExtendedCrate encoder has the same missing Equal-LHS validation for in-memory domain values. A direct `WherePredicateDecl { lhs: "T", rhs: ["u32"], operator: Equal }` passes this branch and emits an `EqPredicate` corresponding to invalid Rust (`where T = u32`), even though the JSON decoder rejects that shape. Since this public adapter already defensively validates Equal RHS cardinality for direct domain inputs, it should also reject non-projection Equal LHS values before emitting `WherePredicate::EqPredicate`.

Dropping `validate_supported_bound` makes `use<...>` look accepted at the codec boundary, but the value still round-trips as a placeholder `GenericBound::TraitBound` with path `use<U>`. Phase 1.5/1.6 treats any unresolved local `ResolvedPath` as an unresolved type reference and will raise `UnresolvedTypeRef`, so this does not actually support precise-capture bounds end-to-end. The new tests only assert `encode()` succeeds, which misses that downstream failure.

`run()` now hard-fails on an `InherentImplDeclV2` whose `type_name` is not in `local_name_to_id`, but the design note in this same block says these entries should be skipped defensively. As written, one stray or external inherent impl will abort the whole codec run instead of being ignored.

The new evaluator tests only assert `!is_red()` at both [signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs:3177](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs:3177) and [signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs:3368](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs:3368). That leaves room for a Yellow regression to pass, even though the brief says these cases should stay Blue.

The new inherent-impl path calls `encode_method_items` without passing the enclosing impl generics, and `encode_method_items` itself only rewrites types against `method.generics`. Any method inside `impl<T> ...` or `trait Foo<T>` that mentions an outer `T` will be encoded as an unresolved path instead of `Type::Generic`, so generic methods will miscompare in phase 2.

The inherent-impl loop silently skips entries whose `type_name` is missing from `local_name_to_id`. That drops catalogue data without surfacing an error, so a malformed `inherent_impls` entry can disappear from the output and the caller never learns the codec input was inconsistent.

`encode_trait` now records trait-level generics on the trait item, but it still calls `encode_method_items(..., &[])`. That means any trait method that mentions a trait parameter, e.g. `trait Foo<T> { fn get(&self) -> T; }`, will encode `T` as an unresolved type in the method signature and fail A/C structural comparison for valid generic traits.

`encode_method_items` now uses outer impl generic names for parameter/return parsing, but `build_where_form_generics` still receives only `method_generic_names`. For valid methods like `impl<T> Foo<T> { fn make<U: Into<T>>() {} }`, the `T` inside `U`'s bound will still be encoded as an unresolved type instead of an in-scope generic, so the encoded `Function.generics` will not match rustdoc.

These new end-to-end cases only cover the happy path (`impl<T: Clone>` on both sides) plus the legacy empty-A case. They never assert that a changed `Impl.generics` shape becomes non-Blue, so a regression that keeps ignoring impl-block generics would still pass this suite. Add a negative mismatch case here.

The codec tests verify generic-parameter counts and names, but they do not check the actual bound payloads emitted by `build_where_form_generics`. For example, `test_trait_decl_generics_encoded_correctly` would still pass if the inline `Clone` bound or the explicit `where T: Send` predicate were dropped. That leaves the new generic/where-form encoding insufficiently covered.

`inherent_impls` are accepted for any `TypeName`, but the append logic only handles `Struct` and `Enum` items. If a catalogue points an inherent impl at a `TypeAlias` (or any future non-struct/enum type), the codec silently leaves the impl orphaned instead of rejecting it. That produces an impossible Rust item in the output and can skew downstream signal evaluation; this should fail closed with an error.

P1: `encode_trait` still serializes `supertrait_bounds` with `encode_bound_str` at [libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec.rs:1461](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec.rs#L1461), so trait generics are not in scope there. A valid declaration like `trait Foo<T>: Into<T>` will encode `T` as an unresolved/path type instead of `Type::Generic`, which breaks the new trait-generic support for any generic supertrait bounds.

P1: The new coverage does not actually pin down the new impl-linking behavior. The inherent-impl test at [libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec_tests.rs:1948](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec_tests.rs#L1948) only checks that some generic `Impl` exists; it never asserts that the generated impl id was appended to the owning type's `impls` list. Likewise, the signal tests at [libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs:3177](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs#L3177) and [libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs:3369](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs#L3369) only assert that the final signal is Blue, which would still pass even if the codec stopped emitting `impl_generics`. That leaves the critical Phase-1 linkage path unprotected.

P1: The new inherent-impl codec test only checks `generics.params` and linkage into `Struct.impls`; it never asserts `impl_inner.generics.where_predicates`. A regression in `InherentImplDeclV2.impl_where_predicates` translation would still pass this test, so the new public path is not actually covered end-to-end.

P1: The backward-compat test for legacy trait impls only asserts the evaluator returns Blue. It does not verify the A-side `Impl` still encodes empty `generics.params` / `where_predicates`, and the evaluator intentionally ignores impl-block generics in structural comparison. That means this regression test would still pass even if old catalogues started emitting impl generics again.

`for_` is external catalogue input describing a Rust type path, but decode copies it straight into `TraitImplDeclV2` without any domain/type validation. Inputs such as `""`, whitespace, or syntactically invalid paths will now be accepted as `Some(...)` and preserved by encode, allowing an illegal catalogue state to flow downstream once this field is consumed. Validate the value before assignment, for example with a dedicated domain type or the existing type-syntax validation path, and add error-case tests for invalid `for_` values.

`encode_for_path` treats the segment before the first `::` as a crate name, so valid Rust paths that start with `crate::`, `self::`, or `super::` are misclassified as external crates. A same-crate `for_` value like `crate::LocalType` will register a fake external crate entry instead of preserving the local-path semantics, which breaks round-trip behavior for syntactically valid inputs.

`try_build_qualified_equal_lhs` only accepts `<IDENT as IDENT>::IDENT` where the self type is one of `generic_names`, and it rejects any qualified Equal LHS with `Self`, concrete self types, or generic args on the trait/associated type. That leaves the new `BoundOp::Equal` path unable to encode several valid Rust projections even though the decoder only requires a `::`-qualified LHS, so encode/decode symmetry is still broken for legitimate inputs.

`try_build_qualified_equal_lhs` only accepts `<Generic as Trait>::Assoc` or `<Self as Trait>::Assoc`. Valid equality predicates whose self type is a concrete path still fail to encode, e.g. `<Vec<T> as Iterator>::Item = U` or `<crate::foo::Bar as Trait>::Assoc = U`. That leaves the new `Equal` support incomplete and still breaks encoder/decoder symmetry for public `WherePredicateDecl` input.

`encode_for_path()` now allocates a fresh synthetic `Id` for same-crate overrides instead of reusing the existing local type id. That means a `for_` like `crate::Foo`/`domain::Foo` no longer points at `Foo`'s real item id, so downstream Phase 1 code that keys off `impl.for_.id` can no longer associate the impl with the owning type.

The new `for_` parser only checks for the presence of `::`. Inputs such as `domain::` or `crate::` therefore pass validation and are encoded with an empty short name / path segment, producing bogus `Crate::paths` entries instead of an `InvalidTypeRef` error.

`encode_qualified_equal_lhs` still stores the trait segment as a raw unresolved `Path` string. For a common input like `<T as Iterator>::Item`, phase 1 later treats `Iterator` as a local unresolved path and raises `UnresolvedTypeRef`, so the new Equal-LHS support still fails after encoding. The trait path needs the same parsing/external-crate resolution used elsewhere.

`encode_for_path` takes the last `::` segment verbatim as the type name, which drops the `Path.args` structure. Valid targets such as `crate::Foo<T>` or `serde::Map<K, V>` are encoded as `Path { path: "Foo<T>", args: None }`, so generic `for_` targets do not round-trip and same-crate impls can no longer reuse the declared type id.

`encode_for_path` falls back to `alloc_id()` when a same-crate override does not match any `local_name_to_id` entry, so typos like `crate::LcoalType` are silently encoded as a real impl target instead of returning `InvalidTypeRef`. Because that fallback is uncached, repeated uses of the same bad path also get different synthetic ids.

The new `for_` parser only rejects bare names and empty tails; it still accepts malformed qualified paths such as `::Foo` or `domain::::Foo` and will register bogus crate/path entries from them. That leaves `TraitImplDeclV2.for_` under-validated and can encode invalid input as a seemingly valid impl target.

`encode_for_path` silently strips generic arguments from `TraitImplDeclV2.for_` by splitting the last segment on `<` and always emitting `args: None`. A valid override like `crate::Foo<Vec<u32>>` will be encoded as `Foo`, so the impl target loses its type arguments and the `paths` metadata no longer matches the input.

Absolute paths that start with `::` are not rejected here: `find("::")` returns `0`, `root_crate` becomes empty, and the code falls into the external-crate branch with `crate_name == ""`. That creates a bogus empty external crate entry and malformed `paths` data instead of rejecting the input.

The new same-crate `for_` branch strips the path down to `type_name_short` and returns `resolved_path_type(type_id, type_name_short)`, so a valid override like `crate::Foo<T>` or `self::Foo<T>` loses its generic arguments and is encoded as `Foo` with `args: None`. That silently changes the impl target and will mis-encode generic same-crate impls.

`encode_qualified_equal_lhs` parses the trait segment, but it never runs `rewrite_generic_types` on that `Path`. Valid inputs like `<T as Foo<U>>::Assoc = V` will leave `U` as an unresolved path instead of `Type::Generic`, so phase 1 will treat the predicate as referencing an unknown type and reject it. This breaks qualified `Equal` predicates for parameterized traits.

Same-crate overrides are rewritten to `crate::...` before parsing. That changes the raw `Type::ResolvedPath.path` away from the short local spelling used by ordinary impls, and `build_impl_identity_map` uses that raw path as the collision tiebreaker. In a short-name collision between a local impl and an external impl, this can make the codec keep a different impl than rustdoc/S-side.

`encode_qualified_equal_lhs` parses concrete `self_str` and `trait_str` directly with `parse_type_ref_str`, so it never applies the same-crate classification used by `encode_for_path`. Valid predicates like `<domain::Foo as domain::Trait>::Assoc = U` will be treated as an external `domain` crate, which pollutes `external_crates` and misclassifies a same-crate path.

The `Equal` branch still only handles the new `<...>::Assoc` form on the LHS and still unconditionally rejects any RHS that starts with `<`, so valid equality predicates such as `where T::Assoc = <U as Trait>::Assoc2` still cannot be encoded. It also falls back to `parse_type_ref_str` for multi-segment projections like `T::Nested::Assoc`, which loses the `QualifiedPath` shape instead of round-tripping it. The decoder accepts these forms, so encode/decode symmetry is still broken for a valid subset of `Equal` predicates.

`encode_qualified_equal_lhs` takes the substring after `>::` verbatim, but it never trims or validates that segment as an identifier. A syntactically valid input like `<T as Iterator>::Item ` will be encoded with `name = "Item "`, producing a malformed `Type::QualifiedPath` that will not match rustdoc output or downstream identity comparisons. Please trim/validate the assoc segment before constructing the path.

The new same-crate normalization only rewrites the outer path root. If `for_` or a qualified `Equal` LHS contains an explicit current-crate prefix inside generic arguments (for example `domain::Wrapper<domain::Inner>`), `parse_type_ref_str` still treats that nested `domain::...` segment as an external crate and registers a fake `external_crates` entry. That corrupts the encoded `Type` tree for valid same-crate inputs.

`encode_qualified_equal_lhs` accepts qualified `Equal` predicates but does not perform the unresolved same-crate check that `encode_for_path` now does. Inputs like `<domain::Missing as Iterator>::Item = u32` will encode successfully with an unresolved local self-type or trait path and only fail later in phase 1, so invalid catalogue data is no longer rejected at the codec boundary.

[`encode_for_path`]( /home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec.rs#L655) only normalizes `{crate_name}::` prefixes in the same-crate branch. If `ti.for_` points at an external type whose generic arguments mention the local crate (for example `other::Wrapper<domain::Inner>`), those inner `domain::...` paths are passed through unchanged and `parse_type_ref_str` will register the local crate as an external crate, corrupting `external_crates`/`Path.id` for the impl target.

[`encode_qualified_equal_lhs`]( /home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec.rs#L2267) still cannot encode nested qualified self-types even though the new path says concrete self types are supported. It delegates any concrete `self_str` directly to `parse_type_ref_str`, which rejects `qself` syntax, so a valid equality like `<<T as Trait>::Assoc as AnotherTrait>::Item = U` will fail to encode even though the decoder accepts any `Equal` LHS containing `::`.

`replace_crate_prefix_globally` is too broad: `s.replace("{crate_name}::", "crate::")` rewrites every matching substring, not just the top-level crate prefix. Valid paths such as `crate::domain::Foo` or `other::domain::Bar` get mangled to `crate::crate::Foo` / `other::crate::Bar`, so `for_` overrides and qualified `Equal` LHS paths can be encoded with the wrong raw path and drift from downstream identity/resolution logic.

`replace_crate_prefix_globally` only treats `<`, `(`, space, and `[` as valid start delimiters for a crate prefix. That misses common Rust syntax boundaries like `,`, `=`, `&`, `*`, and `+`, so nested same-crate refs in inputs such as `Vec<domain::A,domain::B>` or `Iterator<Item=domain::T>` will not be normalized and can still be encoded as fake external `domain` references. This breaks the new `for_`/qualified-Equal path handling for valid type strings.

`replace_crate_prefix_globally()` treats any `crate_name::` substring that is not immediately preceded by `:` as a crate-root match. That still rewrites unrelated identifiers such as `mydomain::Foo` when the local crate is `domain`, so valid external crate paths can be corrupted and registered under the wrong crate name.

`encode_qualified_equal_lhs()` uses `find(">::")`, which grabs the first inner delimiter instead of the outermost one. Nested but valid forms like `<<T as Trait>::Assoc as Another>::Item` are therefore split incorrectly and rejected, even though the helper claims to support nested qualified paths.

`replace_crate_prefix_globally` loses the original `::` context after each replacement, so a later `domain::` that was originally preceded by `::` can be rewritten as a fresh crate root. A valid same-crate path such as `domain::domain::Foo` (or the same pattern inside generic arguments) is therefore normalized to `crate::crate::Foo`, which is not the canonical local-path shape the codec is trying to preserve.

`encode_for_path` only validates the top-level `ResolvedPath.id` and never walks `args`, so undeclared same-crate types nested inside generic arguments still encode successfully. For example, `domain::Outer<domain::Missing>` will leave `Missing` as an unresolved marker instead of failing fast, which violates the documented invariant that unknown same-crate `for_` targets are rejected.

`encode_for_path()` now scans the parsed same-crate path for `UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID` before `rewrite_generic_types()` runs. That means any valid impl target that mentions a method-level generic, such as `impl<T> Trait for domain::Foo<T>` or `crate::Wrapper<T>`, is treated as invalid because `parse_type_ref_str()` represents `T` as unresolved until the later rewrite step. This regresses the generic-impl case the new helper is supposed to support; the unresolved-type check needs to happen after generic rewriting or explicitly ignore `generic_names`.

`BoundOp::Equal` now reaches `encode_qualified_equal_lhs`, but that helper still does not validate bare same-crate self/trait names against `local_name_to_id`. Inputs like `<Missing as Trait>::Assoc = U` or `<LocalType as MissingTrait>::Assoc = U` can be encoded successfully with `UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID` paths instead of failing at the codec boundary, so the new qualified-`Equal` support is not fail-closed.

`BoundOp::Equal` still does not handle bare projection paths rooted at `Self` (for example `Self::Assoc = U`). Those inputs pass the new `contains("::")` gate, but they fall through to `parse_type_ref_str`, which encodes them as `ResolvedPath(Self::Assoc)` instead of a `Type::QualifiedPath`. That leaves a mis-modeled equality predicate in the crate data instead of rejecting or normalizing it.

`encode_qualified_equal_lhs` looks for the literal substring `" as "`, so it rejects qualified Equal predicates that the JSON decoder accepts after `syn::Type` validation, such as `<T  as Iterator>::Item` or forms with newlines/tabs around `as`. This reintroduces a decode-success/encode-fail round-trip hole for valid Rust type syntax; parse the qualified path with `syn` or make the split whitespace-insensitive.

The associated type name after `>::` is used without trimming or identifier validation. A valid Rust spelling like `<T as Iterator>:: Item` passes the decoder's `syn::Type` validation, but this code emits `Type::QualifiedPath { name: " Item", ... }`, causing structural mismatches instead of encoding `Item`. Trim and validate the segment, or derive it from the parsed `syn` AST.

D2 gives `InherentImplDeclV2` its own entry type and simultaneously says `TypeEntry` must not carry impl data, but it never says where the new entry is stored in `CatalogueDocument`. In the current catalogue shape the only impl carrier is `TypeEntry.trait_impls`, so this leaves inherent impl blocks without a serialization/evaluation home.

The same D2 section reuses `MethodGenericParam` for impl- and trait-declaration generics, but that type only models a parameter name plus bounds for type parameters. It cannot represent lifetime or const generic parameters, so the advertised support for impl/trait generic declarations is narrower than the Rust syntax the ADR claims to cover.

D2 says the catalogue will model impl-block and trait-declaration generics, but the proposed fields reuse `Vec<MethodGenericParam>`. That type only represents a named type parameter plus bounds; it cannot encode lifetime parameters like `<'a>` or const parameters like `<const N: usize>`. As written, the plan still cannot represent valid Rust impl/trait generic declarations despite claiming to make this level of the schema Rust-syntax-permissive.

D2's TraitEntry example models `trait Iterator<Item: Clone>` as a generic parameter (`generics: [{ name: "Item", ... }]`), but `Iterator::Item` is an associated type, not a trait type parameter. This puts an associated-type declaration in the trait-generic field and contradicts the stated D2 purpose of representing declarations like `trait Foo<T> where T: ...`; an implementer following the example would add the wrong schema surface for this Rust construct.

`plan.md` assigns T007-T009 signal-evaluator comparison/pre-step work to `Layer: infrastructure` (see lines 34, 42-43), but `spec.md` line 37 / CN-02 says signal evaluator orchestration must live in `libs/usecase/` and infrastructure is only for rustdoc parsing/codec. The layer assignment is inconsistent with the declared architecture.

`task-coverage.json` line 36 omits `T007` from `AC-08`, but `impl-plan.json` line 34 and `plan.md` line 34 explicitly tie T007 to the 'existing catalogue JSON does not change signal evaluation' check. The acceptance-coverage matrix no longer matches the implementation plan.

This contract-map is still only a placeholder: it explicitly suppresses node shapes and edges, so it does not render the cross-layer type relationships that the briefing says must be captured in this artifact. The `IN-24 / OS-07` deferral note is also a stray cross-reference for this track’s spec set.

`contract-map.md` is still a placeholder rather than a rendered contract map: it explicitly says no node shapes or edges are emitted, so the file does not satisfy the scope requirement that this view express the inter-layer type relationships in mermaid form.

P1 contradiction: `IN-01` claims the new `WherePredicateDecl` shape can also declare precise capture (`use<'a, T>`), but the same item only models `BoundOp` as `:` or `=`. With no `Use`/equivalent variant or alternate representation, the schema cannot satisfy the acceptance criterion as written.

Rendered view mismatch: `/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/make-catalogue-schema-permissive-2026-05-19/domain-types.md` marks `BoundOp` and `InherentImplDeclV2` as `—`, but the source `/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/make-catalogue-schema-permissive-2026-05-19/domain-types.json` sets both to `action: "add"`. The markdown view therefore contradicts the generated JSON and obscures the intended add-vs-modify status.

T001 claims `#[serde(default)]` is enough to keep old `type_/bounds` catalogue JSON loadable after renaming the fields to `lhs/rhs/operator`, but defaulting missing fields does not preserve the old values. As written, existing documents would deserialize with empty predicates unless the plan also requires `alias`/`rename` handling or a migration layer.

T001's compatibility claim is incomplete: it only aliases the legacy `type_`/`bounds` fields to `lhs`/`rhs`, but does not provide any legacy mapping or default for the new `operator` field. As written, older `WherePredicateDecl` records still cannot deserialize into the new shape, so the stated backward-compatibility path is not actually sufficient.

T007 requires SignalEvaluatorV2 to add symmetric comparison logic for IN-09, but the SignalEvaluatorV2 type declaration only lists the D1 and D3 changes and its spec_refs stop at IN-03/IN-04/IN-10/IN-11. This makes the infrastructure type plan contradict the implementation plan: the evaluator change needed for impl-block and trait-declaration generics is not declared in the type artifact.

T004 lists `type_name: TypeName` as a required field for the new InherentImplDeclV2 entry, then says every new field should use `#[serde(default)]` with an empty Vec default. That instruction is semantically invalid for `type_name` and would either be unimplementable as written or allow an inherent impl entry without its target type; only the collection fields or the enclosing document collection should default.

The domain type artifact declares the new InherentImplDeclV2 entry, but it does not declare the companion CatalogueDocument modification needed to make that entry appear in catalogue documents, even though T004 explicitly requires adding `CatalogueDocument`'s `inherent_impls` entry. As written, the plan artifacts disagree on whether the document schema itself changes, which undermines AC-05's claim that InherentImplDeclV2 can be declared in a catalogue.

`T011` is the integration checkpoint, but the `tasks` array still orders it before `T012`, even though `T011` says it validates the D4/orphan-guard fix from `T012` and AC-12. Any consumer that follows the JSON task order will schedule integration before the dependent fix exists, which makes the plan ordering internally inconsistent.

CN-01 says every new field on `InherentImplDeclV2` / `TraitImplDeclV2` / `TraitEntry` uses an empty `Vec`/array default, but the same plan introduces non-collection fields such as `WherePredicateDecl.operator` and `InherentImplDeclV2.type_name` with different defaults. As written, the constraint conflicts with the schema changes it is meant to govern.

S2 claims T004-T006 can be started in parallel, but T004 and T006 both touch `libs/domain/.../entries.rs` (`InherentImplDeclV2`/`CatalogueDocument.inherent_impls` vs `TraitEntry.generics`/`where_predicates`). That is not a disjoint write set, so the parallelism claim is infeasible as written.

T011 is only gated on T001-T010 here, but the same plan says the remaining infrastructure red is resolved by D4/T012. As written, the integration step can start before the orphan-guard fix it is meant to verify, so the task order is internally inconsistent; T011 should depend on T012 as well.

T012 / IN-13 / AC-12 rely on a `TraitImplDeclV2.for_` field that can carry a fully-qualified self-type path, but `domain-types.json` only declares `trait_name`, `origin_crate`, `generic_args`, `impl_generics`, and `impl_where_predicates` for `TraitImplDeclV2`. As written, the D4 cross-crate impl cannot be represented in the catalogue, so the task is infeasible unless the schema adds a target-type field or redefines an existing one.

`IN-13`/`T012` require the A-codec to parse `TraitImplDeclV2.for_` as a fully-qualified external self-type and register `external_crates`/`paths`, but the `CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodec` type declaration only records D1/D2 work (`IN-02`, `IN-09`) and its docs say the public shape is otherwise unchanged. The component that must implement the D4 codec behavior is therefore not declared against `IN-13`, while `SignalEvaluatorV2` is. This leaves the D4 plan internally incomplete: the track can declare the new field and remove the evaluator filter while the required codec behavior is missing from the infrastructure type contract.

`AC-10` still requires the external-target `for_is_external` flag to be correctly determined, but `AC-12`/`T012` require deleting the `build_impl_identity_map` `for_is_external` filter block, and `T011` validates all acceptance criteria together. As written, the final plan asks implementers to both keep/test a flag-specific behavior and remove the filter that computes it. Scope `AC-10` to the stable remap guarantee (external `for_` is not overwritten) or mark the flag check as pre-D4 only.

ADR D4 says cross-crate impl declarations can be supported “catalogue schema を変更せず” within the existing `TraitImplDeclV2` structure, but the plan/spec require adding a new `TraitImplDeclV2.for_` field for the same D4 work (`IN-13`/`T012`). That is a semantic contradiction: implementers cannot tell whether D4 is meant to preserve the schema or modify the domain/DTO schema with a new field.

D4/T012 is not wired into the backward-compatibility and architecture constraint coverage. The spec/plan says T012 adds `TraitImplDeclV2.for_` with `serde default None` and updates the A-codec/signal evaluator, so it is subject to CN-01 and CN-02, but `task-coverage.json` lists CN-01 only for T001/T004/T005/T006 and CN-02 only for T002/T003/T007/T008/T009. That lets the D4 schema/evaluator work be tracked as complete without the stated compatibility/layering constraints being applied to it. Add T012 to the relevant constraint coverage, and likely AC-08 if existing catalogues without `for_` are part of the no-version-bump guarantee.

IN-13/T012 add `TraitImplDeclV2.for_` as an `Option<String>` with `serde default None` and describe it as part of the backward-compatible/no-version-bump design, but the spec’s compatibility guardrails still only cover D1/D2/D3 or new `Vec` fields. OS-04 omits D4, CN-01 is worded only for `Vec` fields, and AC-08 lists missing `impl_generics`/`impl_where_predicates`/`TraitEntry.generics` but not missing `for_`, while those same entries map to T012. This leaves the D4 schema addition outside the stated compatibility criteria it is supposed to satisfy.

`T012` is marked `done` here even though the same plan still says it must start only after `T009` completes, and `T009` is still `todo`. That makes the artifact internally inconsistent: a downstream task is shown finished while its stated prerequisite is not.

This scope was described as a track-transition where only T001 should move to `in_progress`, with all other task statuses left unchanged. Instead, the source data now marks T012 as `done` and the rendered plan shows `1/12 resolved`, which overstates progress for this review scope.

The plan still states that T012 implements A-codec parsing for `TraitImplDeclV2.for_` and registers external crates/paths, but the briefing’s accepted deviation says that `for_` parsing is deferred to Phase 2 and this track is limited to catalogue declaration plus DTO backward compatibility. That contradiction makes the current T012/T011 integration state misleading: readers will expect parser/evaluator behavior that is explicitly out of scope for this supplement.

The S4 narrative still says T012 will implement A-codec parsing for `for_` and that this produces symmetric S/C fingerprints, but the T012 task description now explicitly defers that A-codec parsing to Phase 2 and limits this track to catalogue declaration, DTO compatibility, and C-side filter removal. This leaves the plan with conflicting implementation scope for the same task; update the section summary to match the deferred Phase 2 scope.
